questions.specification:
The table below lists the characteristics of four different smart materials.

| Material | Triggering Stimulus | Observed Response |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **W** | UV radiation | Reversible change in colour |
| **X** | Mechanical stress | Generation of a small electric voltage |
| **Y** | Thermal energy | Reversion to a pre-programmed shape |
| **Z** | Physical pressure | Significant drop in electrical resistance |

Which material in the table displays the properties of a **Shape Memory Alloy (SMA)**?

A product designer is selecting a material for a smartphone screen. Which mechanical property is the primary requirement for ensuring the surface resists scratching from everyday objects such as keys or coins?

The production of gold leaf involves hammering the metal into extremely thin, continuous sheets for use in decorative arts. Which mechanical property of gold allows it to be shaped in this way without fracturing?

A product designer is choosing a material for the outer casing of a premium smartphone. They require a material that is highly resistant to surface scratches and permanent indentation from everyday contact with keys and coins. Which property is the designer primarily seeking?

A material scientist is comparing the mechanical failure modes of four different industrial components during a standardized stress test. The observations from the failure analysis are summarized in the table below:

| Component | Primary Observation at Fracture |
| :--- | :--- |
| Component 1 | The sample underwent substantial plastic flow and thinning before the final rupture. |
| Component 2 | The component failed abruptly without any visible signs of yielding or stretching. |
| Component 3 | The part experienced a slow, continuous change in shape while held under a steady load. |
| Component 4 | The material exhibited a linear and fully reversible strain response to the applied load. |

Which component's behavior is most characteristic of a **brittle** material?

options.markscheme: This describes a ductile material, which undergoes significant plastic deformation before breaking.
Brittle materials are characterized by little to no plastic deformation (yielding) before sudden failure or fracture.
This describes the property of creep, which is time-dependent deformation under constant stress.
This describes elastic behavior, where the material returns to its original state when the load is removed.

questions.specification:
An industrial designer is selecting a material for the protective lens of a high-end camera designed for use in abrasive environments. The lens must be highly resistant to surface scratching and permanent indentation.

| Material | Hardness Rating (1–10) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Material W | 3.5 |
| Material X | 5.5 |
| Material Y | 7.0 |
| Material Z | 9.0 |

Based on the property data in the table, which material is most suitable for this application?

A manufacturer is designing a safety spoon for infants that changes from blue to pink when the food is above a certain temperature. Which smart material is integrated into the polymer to provide this functional property?

The manufacturing of high-performance mountain bikes requires a precise combination of advanced material selection and rigorous production management to ensure safety and performance under extreme conditions.

![Bicycle](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-physics-new-f7aac141-b41a-456e-8f55-19f43d12c7f3-1771929956947/9112d62d-adba-4a37-8e40-ce350e2c4524.jpeg)

parts.content: Identify **two** mechanical properties that are essential for the high-tension steel used in the spokes of a bicycle wheel.
parts.markscheme:
- High tensile strength (to withstand the constant pulling forces without snapping) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- High ductility (to allow the spoke ends to be bent and threaded during manufacturing without becoming brittle) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain **two** ways in which **quality checks during manufacturing** and **final inspection/testing** differ in the production of high-safety bicycle components.
parts.markscheme:
### Quality checks during manufacturing:

- Focus on monitoring and controlling the process as components are being made (e.g., checking jig alignment, measuring tube wall thickness during machining) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Aims to identify and correct variation early to reduce defects and rework (e.g., adjusting settings if weld penetration is out of tolerance) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Uses in-process measurements/sampling to keep production within specification <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Final inspection/testing:

- Focus on assessing the completed component/product against the specification after manufacture (e.g., dimensional inspection of a finished fork, load testing of a wheel) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Confirms conformity and screens out non-conforming items before dispatch to customers <marks>1 mark</marks>
- May include destructive or non-destructive tests to verify performance and safety (e.g., fatigue testing, dye penetrant testing of welds) <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each of two distinct points in an explanation of the role/difference of quality checks during manufacturing or final inspection/testing up to <marks>3 marks max</marks> per explanation.</note>

<note>Do not award marks across clusters.</note>

<note>Mark as <marks>3 marks</marks> + <marks>3 marks</marks>.</note>

parts.content: Explain **one** way the use of **standardized parts** assists in the maintenance and repair of a mountain bike.
parts.markscheme:
- Standardized parts utilize universal dimensions and interfaces (e.g., standard bolt sizes or tire diameters) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This ensures that replacement components from different manufacturers are compatible and interchangeable <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Which simplifies the repair process for the user and reduces the variety of tools required for maintenance <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each of three distinct points in an explanation of how standardized parts assist maintenance up to <marks>3 marks max</marks>.</note>

parts.content: Explain how **craft production**, **mass production**, and **mass customization** are applied to different segments of the bicycle industry.
parts.markscheme:
**Craft production:**

- Refers to small-scale production where highly skilled workers create unique or bespoke products <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Custom frame builders use this method to tailor geometry and material selection to a specific rider's physical dimensions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This results in a high-cost, high-quality product with a very long lead time but perfect user fit <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Mass production:**

- Involves the high-volume manufacture of standardized products using assembly lines and specialized machinery <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Budget and entry-level mountain bikes are produced this way to take advantage of economies of scale <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This makes cycling accessible to a wider market by significantly lowering the unit cost of each bicycle <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Mass customization:**

- A strategy that uses flexible computer-integrated manufacturing systems to produce custom-output at mass-production prices <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Online platforms allow customers to choose specific colors, drivetrain components, and wheelsets for a standard frame model <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This combines the efficiency of large-scale manufacturing with the consumer desire for a personalized product <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each of three distinct points in an explanation for each of the three production strategies up to <marks>3 marks max</marks> for each.</note>

<note>Do not award marks across clusters.</note>

<note>Mark as <marks>3 marks</marks> + <marks>3 marks</marks> + <marks>3 marks</marks>.</note>
